title: CoQuIR: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Code Quality-Aware Information Retrieval
abstract: Code retrieval is essential in modern software development, as it boosts code reuse and accelerates debugging. However, current benchmarks primarily emphasize functional relevance while neglecting critical dimensions of software quality. Motivated by this gap, we introduce CoQuIR, the first large-scale, multilingual benchmark specifically designed to evaluate quality-aware code retrieval across four key dimensions: correctness, efficiency, security, and maintainability. CoQuIR provides fine-grained quality annotations for 42,725 queries and 134,907 code snippets in 11 programming languages, and is accompanied by two quality-centric evaluation metrics: Pairwise Preference Accuracy and Margin-based Ranking Score. Using CoQuIR, we benchmark 23 retrieval models, covering both open-source and proprietary systems, and find that even top-performing models frequently fail to distinguish buggy or insecure code from their more robust counterparts. Furthermore, we conduct preliminary investigations into training methods that explicitly encourage retrievers to recognize code quality. Using synthetic datasets, we demonstrate promising improvements in quality-aware metrics across various models, without sacrificing semantic relevance. Downstream code generation experiments further validate the effectiveness of our approach. Overall, our work highlights the importance of integrating quality signals into code retrieval systems, laying the groundwork for more trustworthy and robust software development tools.
extracted_text:
CoQuIR: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Code
Quality-Aware Information Retrieval
Jiahui Geng1∗
Fengyu Cai2∗
Shaobo Cui3
Qing Li1
Liangwei Chen4
Chenyang Lyu1
Haonan Li1
Derui Zhu5
Alexander Pretschner5
Heinz Koeppl2
Fakhri Karray1
1Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence (MBZUAI), UAE
2Technical University of Darmstadt, Germany
3École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Switzerland
4Google Tokyo, Japan
5Technical University of Munich (TUM), Germany
Abstract
Code retrieval is essential in modern software development as it boosts reuse and
speeds up debugging. However, current benchmarks primarily emphasize func-
tional relevance while neglecting critical dimensions of software quality. Motivated
by this gap, we introduce CoQuIR, the first large-scale, multilingual benchmark
specifically designed to evaluate quality-aware code retrieval across four critical di-
mensions: correctness, efficiency, security, and maintainability. CoQuIR includes
fine-grained quality annotations over 42,725 queries and 134,907 code snippets
in 11 programming languages and is accompanied by two quality-centric evalu-
ation metrics (Pairwise Preference Accuracy and Margin-based Ranking Score).
Based on CoQuIR, we benchmark 23 retrieval models, spanning open-source and
proprietary models, and find that even top-performing models often fail to distin-
guish buggy or insecure code from their robust counterparts. Furthermore, we
conduct preliminary investigations into methods for explicitly training retrievers to
recognize code quality. Through synthetic datasets, we demonstrate promising im-
provements in quality-aware metrics across different models without compromising
semantic relevance. Downstream code generation performance further validates
the effectiveness of our approach. Our work underscores the importance of incor-
porating quality signals into code retrieval systems, establishing a foundation for
more trustworthy software development tools.
1
Introduction
Code retrieval is crucial for code generation by introducing relevant code snippets as references for
large language model (LLM) generation, significantly advancing automated software development [1,
2, 3, 4, 5]. However, existing benchmarks primarily inherit classical criteria from natural language
retrieval, focusing on semantic or functional relevance, i.e., whether the retrieval code addresses the
intent of a query. These benchmarks largely overlook critical, code-specific quality dimensions such
as correctness, efficiency, security, and maintainability [6, 7, 8, 9].
Ignoring these crucial properties of code introduces significant negative impacts on downstream
applications. As illustrated in Figure 1, functionally relevant but low-quality code, such as snippets
*Equal contribution.
§ https://github.com/TRUMANCFY/CoQuIR
https://huggingface.co/CoQuIR
Preprint. Under Review
arXiv:2506.11066v2  [cs.SE]  27 Aug 2025


containing subtle bugs, deprecated APIs, or security vulnerabilities, can still be ranked highly by
current retrieval models. Incorporating such flawed examples into downstream tasks, e.g., retrieval-
augmented generation (RAG), can propagate technical debt or introduce exploitable vulnerabilities.
These issues often remain hidden as they may not directly affect input-output correctness, yet become
harmful in the long run, while detecting them manually or via static analysis is both costly and
time-consuming.
Figure 1: CoQuIR’s core idea: a retriever
should rank high-quality code above low-
quality counterparts.
These challenges underscore the urgent need for re-
trieval systems that go beyond surface-level func-
tional matching to explicitly account for code quality,
leading to a central research question:
Can code retrievers be designed
based on not only relevance but
also quality?
To address this gap, we introduce Code Quality-
aware Information Retrieval (CoQuIR), a benchmark
specifically tailored for quality-aware code retrieval.
CoQuIR is the first comprehensive resource that ex-
plicitly annotates code snippets across four critical
quality dimensions: (1) Correctness: whether the
code is bug-free or contains errors; (2) Efficiency:
whether the implementation is efficient or introduces
unnecessary performance overhead; (3) Security:
whether it avoids known vulnerabilities or contains
insecure patterns; and (4) Maintainability: whether
the code uses stable, recommended APIs or relies on deprecated or poor-practice constructs. Repre-
sentative queries and annotated code candidates are shown in Figure 2. CoQuIR spans 11 widely used
programming languages and pairs natural language queries with multiple code candidates serving as
contrastive examples. It is constructed from high-quality code datasets originally curated for tasks
such as code pretraining, vulnerability detection, and instruction tuning [10, 11, 12, 13]. All code
snippets are sourced from real-world projects, ensuring the benchmark accurately reflects practical
development scenarios.
Using CoQuIR, we comprehensively evaluate over 20 retrieval models, which covers six paradigms:
(1) unsupervised retrievers [14]); (2) supervised retrievers [15, 16]; (3) code-specific retrievers [17,
18]; (4) LLM-based retrievers [15, 19, 20]; (5) instruction-following retrievers [19, 21, 22]; and
(6) proprietary API-based models [23, 24]. We assess model performance using both traditional
relevance-based metrics and two newly introduced quality-aware metrics: Pairwise Preference
Accuracy (PPA) and Margin-based Ranking Score (MRS). These metrics are designed to evaluate a
model’s ability to distinguish high-quality implementations from suboptimal ones. Our results reveal
substantial challenges CoQuIR poses, showing that current state-of-the-art models often struggle to
distinguish high-quality code from flawed implementations.
Beyond the benchmark, we further investigate approaches to explicitly teaching retrievers to become
quality-aware. To achieve this, we automatically construct a large-scale, contrastive training corpus
annotated with all four critical dimensions of code quality. Empirical evaluations demonstrate robust
gains in quality-aware metrics (e.g., ∼20–30% in PPA), surpassing all the benchmarked models, in-
cluding proprietary APIs, without compromising functional relevance. Besides, downstream analysis
in RAG further confirms that integrating quality-aware retrieval effectively reduces vulnerabilities,
which highlights the practical value of our approach for building robust software systems.
In summary, our main contributions are threefold: (1) We introduce CoQuIR, the first benchmark for
code retrieval annotated with four essential software quality dimensions, i.e., correctness, efficiency,
security, and maintainability, enabling a systematic evaluation of models’ quality-awareness beyond
functional relevance. (2) We comprehensively evaluate over 20 popular retrieval models spanning
diverse paradigms, using both traditional relevance-based metrics and newly proposed quality-aware
metrics. Our results reveal critical limitations in the current models’ ability to distinguish and
prioritize high-quality code effectively. (3) We develop a contrastive-training method designed to
improve the quality-awareness of retrieval models. This method is further validated to achieve

substantial performance gains not only in retrieval accuracy but also in downstream code generation
tasks, thus paving the way toward more trustworthy software engineering toolchains.
2
Related Work
Modern dense retrievers
Dense retrievers encode queries and documents into a shared embedding
space, enabling semantic retrieval beyond lexical overlap. Early encoder-based models [25, 26, 27]
are trained contrastively with hard negatives to capture deeper semantics. As retrieval systems are
applied to increasingly complex scenarios, recent work has focused on enhancing their reasoning
and instruction-following capabilities. Su et al.[28] introduced a benchmark requiring intensive
reasoning, while Faltings et al.[29] proposed extracting logical structures from queries to support
complex reasoning. Additionally, instruction-following retrievers are evaluated in FollowIR [30] and
further improved via GPT-generated instruction tuning [22]. Zhao et al.[31] model latent user intent
such as support or oppose, highlighting the need for nuanced intent understanding. These works
inspire us to also target more complex retrieval, but from a novel scenario, i.e., code retrieval. We
extend the focus beyond semantic or functional relevance to fine-grained distinctions among retrieved
code snippets, specifically code quality in our scope.
Code retrieval benchmarks
Code retrieval datasets and benchmarks have evolved significantly
to address the growing complexity of semantic matching between queries and codebases. Early
efforts like CodeSearchNet [6] focused on pairing code functions with natural language comments
across six programming languages. CoSQA[32] moved toward more realistic evaluation by aligning
over 20,000 natural language queries with relevant code snippets, thereby simulating practical
search behavior in real-world programming tasks. XcodeEval[33] encompasses both text-to-code
and code-to-code retrieval across multiple languages. More recently, CoIR [9] unified ten existing
datasets and eight retrieval tasks into a comprehensive benchmark, enabling holistic evaluation
across different programming domains and query modalities. However, these benchmarks focus
primarily on functional relevance, overlooking critical software quality dimensions. To address
this, we introduce the first benchmark that explicitly incorporates correctness, efficiency, security,
and maintainability, covering eleven programming languages with quality-annotated code pairs for
comprehensive, quality-aware retrieval evaluation.

3
CoQuIR Benchmark
CoQuIR enables comprehensive quality-aware code retrieval by evaluating correctness, maintain-
ability, efficiency, and security dimensions across eleven programming languages. The benchmark
incorporates contrastive examples with explicit quality annotations, facilitating fine-grained assess-
ment of model discrimination capabilities.
3.1
Benchmark Construction
Our benchmark is constructed from established code-related datasets, organized across distinct quality
dimensions [10, 11, 34, 12, 13, 35]. These datasets have been meticulously curated through static
analysis, rule-based heuristics, and expert validation. Table 1 summarizes the datasets used for each
dimension, while Figure 2 shows representative examples. Additional statistics, including average
query length, corpus size, and language distribution, are detailed in Appendix A.1.
Correctness
We construct CodeNet-B, a comprehensive multilingual dataset derived from Co-
deNet [10]. To ensure robust cross-lingual representation, we select 348 problems containing at
least one correct and one incorrect submission across all 10 target programming languages. For
each problem–language pair, we select up to three positive examples (Accepted submissions with
minimal cpu_time) and three negative examples with typical failure types (e.g., Compile Error,
Wrong Answer, Runtime Error); fewer are used when enough examples are available. To further
ensure statistical validity, we augment the dataset with contrastive examples from Defects4J [11],
matching buggy and fixed Java code. Following [34], we prompt GPT-4o-mini to generate functional
summaries of the fixed implementations, which serve as natural language queries for retrieval.
Efficiency
CodeNet-E follows the same setup as CodeNet-B but defines negative examples as
suboptimal yet correct submissions, i.e., those code implementations with Accepted status and
maximal cpu_time. We select up to three efficient and three inefficient implementations per problem-
language pair, across 511 problems in 10 languages. Additionally, we include SQLR2 [35], a dataset
of functionally equivalent SQL queries with improved execution efficiency. We use GPT-4o-mini to
generate functional summaries of the optimized queries for retrieval.
Security
Our evaluation incorporates security-focused contrastive code examples from two high-
quality datasets. SafeCoder [34] provides an instruction-tuning dataset specifically designed for
secure code generation. Constructed through an automated pipeline that extracts and filters code
snippets from GitHub commits, this dataset covers 23 Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE)
categories across six programming languages. Each example contains functionally equivalent secure
and vulnerable code pairs, alongside natural language instructions generated by GPT-4, which we
utilize as queries. Additionally, we include examples from CVEFixes [12], a dataset of real-world
4


vulnerability fixes in open-source projects. For each patched code sample, we leverage GPT-4o-mini
to summarize its functionality and treat these summaries as queries for retrieving the related code.
Maintainability
To assess the model’s ability to retrieve maintainable code, we employ De-
preAPI [13], a recent dataset designed to evaluate LLM behavior in scenarios involving deprecated
API usage. It contains 145 deprecated-to-recommended API pairs across eight widely-used Python
libraries, including NumPy and Pandas, along with corresponding outdated and updated code imple-
mentations. Unlike typical code completion benchmarks, DepreAPI does not provide explicit prompts
specifying the intended function, which poses additional challenges for retrieval. To construct natural
language queries for our benchmark, we prompt GPT-4o-mini to generate functional summaries of
the original code, explicitly excluding any references to deprecated or updated APIs.
3.2
Evaluation Metrics.
Traditional retrieval metrics
To assess the effectiveness of different retrievers, we primarily use
Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank 10 (nDCG@10) and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR),
both of which emphasize functional relevance. nDCG@10 accounts for the graded relevance
of retrieved results and their positions in the ranking, making it suitable for evaluating nuanced
semantic matching. MRR complements this by measuring the rank of the first relevant result, thereby
highlighting a model’s ability to retrieve functionally relevant code early in the list.
Quality-aware metrics
We introduce two metrics to assess a model’s ability to prioritize high-
quality over low-quality code in a pairwise comparison setting. For each query, we consider a set of
positive samples P = {p1, . . . , pM} and negative samples N = {n1, . . . , nN}.
Pairwise Preference Accuracy (PPA) measures the proportion of positive-negative pairs in which the
model assigns a higher score to the positive sample, using a scoring function s(·)
PPA =
1
|P| · |N|
X
p∈P
X
n∈N
1 (s(p) > s(n)) .
(1)
A PPA of 1 indicates perfect preference for high-quality code, while a score of 0 indicates complete
failure. Under random ranking, the expected PPA is 0.5.
Margin-based Ranking Score (MRS) quantifies the average rank-based margin between positive and
negative samples, using the reciprocal rank r(·)
MRS =
1
|P| · |N|
X
p∈P
X
n∈N
(r(p) −r(n)) .
(2)
In the context of code quality, certain code snippets, such as those containing vulnerabilities, should
be avoided, even if they are functionally relevant. MRS quantifies the extent to which a retriever
prioritizes quality awareness, treating code quality as the primary ranking criterion, which is stricter
than PPA. An ideal retriever, with an MRS approaching 1, ranks high-quality code at the top and
pushes low-quality code to the bottom, even below functionally irrelevant candidates. Conversely,
an MRS near -1 indicates the worst-case behavior: ranking low-quality code highest while ignoring
quality altogether. When the retriever does not have quality-awareness, MRS should be around 0.
4
Experiments and Results
4.1
Experimental Setup
To rigorously benchmark the effectiveness and quality-awareness of retrieval models, we compre-
hensively evaluate various retrievers spanning six representative categories. Detailed descriptions
of all evaluated models are provided in Table 4 in the Appendix. Unsupervised retrievers are
trained without labeled query-document pairs, such as BM25 [36] and Contriever [14]. Supervised
retrievers leverage annotated datasets to learn semantically rich representations, with representative
models including E5 [16], GTR [37], and GTE [15]. Code-specific retrievers are tailored for soft-
ware engineering tasks, trained on code-related query-document pairs to enhance their understanding
of programming semantics; we include CodeSage [38] at two parameter scales and CodeRankEm-
bed [39] in our evaluation. LLM-based retrievers utilize large language model backbones and rely on

retrievers incorporate task-specific instructions into the embedding process, enabling them to adapt
to user intent; we examine seven variants from the Instructor [21] and Promptriever [22] families,
spanning a range of model sizes and architectures. Prompts used for instruction tuning are detailed
in Appendix B.4. Finally, API-based retrievers include proprietary embedding systems accessible
via commercial APIs, such as four models from OpenAI [24] and the Voyage-Code-2/3 series [23],
which are specialized in code search.
4.2
Main Results
Tables 2 and 3 report retrieval performance across models using relevance-based (nDCG@10, MRR)
and quality-aware (PPA, MRS) metrics. In Table 2, darker shades indicate stronger relevance; in
Table 3, scores below/above the random baseline (PPA: 0.5; MRS: 0) are shaded blue/pink. From
these results, we draw the following key findings:
(1) Our benchmark poses challenges for both relevance and quality-aware retrieval.
We
observe significant variability in dataset difficulty under relevance-based metrics. For example, most
models achieve below 50% on both nDCG@10 and MRR for CodeNet-B, CodeNet-E, and SQLR2.
This can be attributed to the fact that these datasets consist of full program implementations rather than
isolated function-level snippets, making retrieval more complex. Moreover, as shown in Table 3, the
benchmark presents additional challenges in quality-aware retrieval. Except for Voyage-code-3, all
models perform below the random baseline (highlighted in blue) on at least one dataset, underscoring
the difficulty of aligning retrieval with real-world quality requirements.
(2) Traditional relevance metrics fail to precisely capture code quality.
While nDCG@10 and
MRR exhibit internal consistency across models and datasets, they fail to reflect distinctions in code
quality. Several models achieve high relevance but low PPA and MRS, especially on security and
maintainability tasks (e.g., SafeCoder, DepreAPI), while SQLR2 shows the opposite trend—low

relevance but strong quality signals—highlighting the limits of relevance-based evaluation. We
compute the Pearson’s correlation between nDCG@10 and PPA across the different retrievers on
these seven tasks. The results are presented in Fig. 9 in Appendix B.2. Though there are some strong
correlations between relevance metrics and quality-aware metrics on CodeNet-B and CVEFixes,
the two measures are poorly associated on SafeCoder and DepreAPI, revealing the distinction of
quality-awareness in code retrieval.
(3) Voyage-code-3 achieves state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks.
Voyage-code-
3 consistently outperforms all other models on both relevance-based and quality-aware metrics across
nearly all datasets. Notably, its MRS scores are often twice as high as those of other strong baselines,
demonstrating its superior ability to prioritize high-quality over low-quality code. This performance
suggests the integration of quality-aligned training objectives and high-quality supervision signals
that go beyond mere semantic matching. The pronounced performance gap further underscores the
importance of explicitly modeling code quality during retriever training.
(4) Larger, instruction-tuned models exhibit stronger quality-awareness.
Model size and archi-
tectural sophistication play a significant role in quality-aware retrieval. LLM-based and instruction-
following retrievers consistently outperform unsupervised and supervised models, particularly in
scenarios requiring subtle quality distinctions. Instruction-tuned models with larger capacities, such
as Pmpretr-8b and Repllama-8b, show a marked advantage in capturing fine-grained quality sig-
nals compared to their smaller counterparts. This trend suggests that larger models, enhanced by
instruction tuning and extensive pretraining, are better at capturing deeper aspects of code quality.
4.3
More Analysis
Retrieval performance varies significantly across programming languages.
We evaluate three
representative models (Repllma-8b, Pmpretr-8b-instr, and Voyage-code-3) on multilingual datasets to
assess their generalization across languages. Figure 3a displays radar charts reporting both relevance-
7


(a)
(b)
Figure 3: The left figure shows retrieval performance across programming languages on CodeNet-B
and SafeCoder. The right figure illustrates sensitivity to instruction variations, measured by PPA (%).
based metrics (nDCG@10, solid lines) and quality-aware metrics (PPA, dashed lines) for CodeNet-B
and SafeCoder. Results for additional datasets are provided in Figure 10a (Appendix). Among
the models, Voyage-code-3 consistently attains the highest nDCG@10 scores, suggesting superior
generalization to diverse code structures. In contrast, Repllma-8b and Pmpretr-8b-instr show greater
performance fluctuations, particularly on low-resource or syntactically distinctive languages such
as Rust and Swift. Notably, the discrepancy between nDCG@10 and PPA underscores that high
relevance scores do not necessarily correlate with high-quality retrieval.
Existing instruction-following retrievers fail to adapt retrieval quality accordingly.
While
prior experiments demonstrate the strong performance of instruction-following retrievers, we further
investigate their sensitivity to variations in instruction semantics. Specifically, we conduct a compara-
tive analysis under three conditions: Original (our primary setting, prompting the model to retrieve
higher-quality code), Without Instruction, and Negative Instruction (prompting the model to retrieve
lower-quality code). The corresponding prompts are detailed in Table 6. As shown in Figure 3b, all
retrievers exhibit minimal differences in both PPA and MRS (Figure 10b in the Appendix) across
these settings. These results indicate that current instruction-tuned retrievers are largely insensitive to
instruction semantics and struggle to adapt retrieval quality based on intent.
5
Teaching Quality Awareness in Code Retrieval
We turn a relevance-only retriever into a quality-aware one in three stages: (1) curate a contrastive
corpus labelled for correctness, efficiency, security, and maintainability; (2) fine-tune retrievers on
this corpus so that high-quality code is ranked higher; (3) verify the gains in both retrieval and
retrieval-augmented generation.
5.1
Empowering Retrievers with Code Quality Awareness
To teach retrievers quality sensitivity, we construct a large-scale contrastive training corpus covering
four dimensions. For correctness and efficiency, we select problems from CodeNet [10] that are not
in CoQuIR, focusing on those with submissions in at least four different programming languages and
containing both positive and negative examples, as defined in CoQuIR. For each language within
a problem, we retain up to eight positive and negative samples. As contrastive data is limited for
security, we employ GPT-4o-mini and Gemini-2.0-Flash to synthesize functionally equivalent code
pairs, with and without specific CWE patterns, based on prompts and CWEs from LLMSecEval [40]
and SecurityEval [41]. We retain only the pairs for which both models produce consistent security
assessments. These models have demonstrated strong performance on code-related tasks [42, 43].
Similarly, we utilize API migration data from DepreAPI to generate functionally equivalent code
using deprecated and modern APIs for maintainability. The correctness of the generated code is
also verified by both LLMs. More details about data statistics are available in Table 7. We fine-tune
Repllama-3b-quality and Repllama-8b-quality on the combined corpus and MS MARCO [44] using
the contrastive retrieval strategy from [20]. Training details are provided in Appendix B.6.
8


Figure 4: Retrieval improvements brought by quality-aware fine-tuning. The left plot illustrates
gains in proposed quality-aware metrics. The right plot shows variations in classic retrieval metrics.
5.2
Performance Evaluation
Impact on code retrieval
Figure 4 demonstrates the substantial impact of quality-awareness
finetuning on retriever performance across multiple benchmarks. When comparing quality-aware
models (Repllama-3b-quality and Repllama-8b-quality) against their standard counterparts (Repllama-
3b and Repllama-8b), we observe consistent improvements across both quality-focused and traditional
retrieval metrics. The quality-aware variants exhibit markedly enhanced PPA and MRS scores,
with improvements of 20-30% across all evaluated datasets. Particularly, MRS shows dramatic
enhancements, increasing from near-zero or negative values to gains exceeding 10% across all
benchmarks, with improvements surpassing 20% on the SafeCoder benchmark. These results surpass
previous state-of-the-art Voyage models. Notably, these improvements are achieved with minimal or
no degradation in classical relevance-based metrics such as nDCG@10 and MRR. This confirms that
incorporating quality-sensitive supervision enables retrievers to prioritize higher-quality code without
sacrificing conventional retrieval utility on relevance.
Figure 5: Quality-aware retrieval cuts vulnerabili-
ties and deprecated-API use in the generated code.
Impact on downstream tasks
We focus on se-
curity and maintainability here. Following [45],
we construct a retrieval corpus containing both
secure and vulnerable implementations, evalu-
ating performance across nine real-world CWE
scenarios, corresponding to the training and eval-
uation split of [46]. We assess the impact of
retrieval quality using the top-5 results from
Repllama-8b, both before and after fine-tuning.
In addition, we evaluate a setting that includes
positive and negative demonstrations, i.e., se-
cure and vulnerable code snippets of the same CWE type and programming language. Across three
independent runs (temperature = 0.4), experiments on CodeGen (2B,[47]), Incoder (6B,[48]), and
Santa (1.1B,[49]) consistently show that quality-aware retrievers reduce the generation of vulnerable
code, as evaluated using CodeQL over 575 outputs (Figure5(a)). Positive demonstrations further am-
plify this effect. Notably, Incoder produces the lowest vulnerability rates across all settings, indicating
strong robustness. For maintainability, we analyze API usage based on the DrepreAI [13], with 20
test cases per API mapping. Under the same setting, fine-tuned retrievers and positive demonstrations
lead to increased usage of updated APIs and reduced reliance on deprecated ones (Figure 5(b)).
Some generated outputs employ alternative implementations that do not invoke either version of the
target API. Again, Incoder demonstrates the highest rate of modern API usage, suggesting superior
adaptability in realistic coding scenarios. More details are available in Appendix B.7.
6
Conclusion
In this work, we introduce CoQuIR, a new benchmark that brings code quality to the forefront
of retrieval evaluation. Unlike prior datasets that focus solely on functional relevance, CoQuIR
provides fine-grained annotations across four quality-centric dimensions: correctness, efficiency,
security, and maintainability. Through extensive experiments on over 20 retrievers, we show that
most current systems lack an explicit preference for high-quality code and often prioritize buggy or
insecure implementations over their robust counterparts. To better capture this behavior, we propose
9


two evaluation metrics that assess a model’s ability to favor high-quality over low-quality code.
Finally, we demonstrate that incorporating quality-aware training signals improves the performance,
underscoring the value of quality-conscious design. We believe that CoQuIR will lay the groundwork
for next-generation code retrieval systems aligned with real-world software engineering needs.
Limitations
However, our study has several limitations. While we cover mainstream programming
languages, other languages remain unexplored and are left for future work. Additionally, CoQuIR
focuses on four quality dimensions and may not fully cover other important code properties (e.g.,
readability, robustness across platforms).
